intro: Amersham is a short drive from our Harefield base, just over the Buckinghamshire border, so we are on site quickly and often. We work across both halves of the town: the timber-framed and Georgian heart of Old Amersham on the Misbourne valley floor, and the substantial Edwardian and Metroland villas of Amersham-on-the-Hill. Most of what we are asked to do here is landscaping, full home renovations and house extensions on the larger detached plots the area is known for.

Building in Amersham means building within the Chilterns National Landscape and the Green Belt, where planning is tight and design has to suit the setting. We handle the applications with Buckinghamshire Council ourselves, from pre-application advice through conservation and AONB consent to building control sign-off. You deal with one contractor from the first drawing to the final clean, not a chain of subcontractors.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Old Amersham's High Street stock: timber-framed, brick-and-flint and Georgian houses, many listed, sitting within the Misbourne conservation area.
Amersham-on-the-Hill grew with the railway as Metroland, leaving substantial Edwardian and interwar villas and 1930s parades around Sycamore Road.
Cavity-brick and mock-Tudor villas on the hill, finished in clay tile, alongside more recent executive homes on generous plots.
Solid-wall and timber-framed period properties that need sympathetic repair rather than off-the-shelf modern methods.

localKnowledge.heading: What Amersham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions, basements and high-spec renovations on the hill villas, worked up to sit comfortably within AONB and Green Belt limits.
Sympathetic repair and alteration of listed and timber-framed homes in Old Amersham, with the conservation consent that goes with them.
Flood and groundwater resilience on valley-floor plots near the River Misbourne, a chalk stream that periodically floods the low ground.
Damp and insulation retrofit in solid-wall period homes, and limescale-driven plumbing work from the very hard chalk-fed water.

whyUs.title: Two builders for two Amershams
whyUs.body: We work confidently in both the listed timber-frame and Georgian fabric of the old town and the Edwardian and Metroland villas on the hill, treating each on its own terms.

whyUs.title: AONB and Green Belt planning handled
whyUs.body: We prepare and run applications through Buckinghamshire Council's conservation, AONB and Green Belt regimes, designing extensions and outbuildings that stand a real chance of consent.

whyUs.title: Heritage repair done properly
whyUs.body: On Old Amersham's solid-wall and timber-framed houses we use breathable, sympathetic methods rather than modern fixes that trap damp in old fabric.

whyUs.title: Built for valley-floor ground
whyUs.body: On low plots near the Misbourne we design for the chalk-stream flooding and high groundwater the valley is known for, rather than ignoring it until it shows.

intro: We are a short run south-west of our Harefield base, so Ascot sits comfortably within our regular working area. We are well used to the SL5 stretch around the Racecourse, Sunningdale and the edge of Windsor Great Park, where the work tends to be high-end landscaping, loft conversions and substantial extensions on large, often wooded plots. We bring a settled team to each site and keep one point of contact from the first survey through to handover.

Ascot brings two things most of our catchment does not. The ground is Bagshot sand rather than London clay, which changes how foundations, drainage and soakaways are designed, and much of the area sits close to protected heathland. We handle the planning and consent process with the council and the relevant heathland considerations directly, so the right approach is agreed before any ground is broken.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Large detached houses and gated estate homes on substantial, often wooded or heathland plots, many at the top of the Berkshire market.
Victorian commuter-era villas dating from the arrival of the railway in 1856, built in brick and render with varied roofs.
High-specification modern builds and replacement dwellings on premium plots around the Racecourse, the golf clubs and Windsor Great Park.
Properties with large grounds where the garden and landscaping carry as much weight as the house itself.

localKnowledge.heading: What Ascot homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions, basements and leisure additions on big plots, designed to sit properly with the existing house.
Foundations, drainage and soakaways on Bagshot sand, which behaves very differently from the clay and chalk found across the rest of our area.
Schemes near Chobham Common, Bagshot Heath and Windsor Great Park, where heathland protection shapes what can be built.
Hard Thames Valley water and the limescale it leaves, addressed during kitchen, bathroom and plumbing work.

whyUs.title: We build for sandy ground
whyUs.body: Ascot sits on free-draining Bagshot sand, not clay, so we design foundations, drainage and soakaways for soft, loose, high-infiltration ground rather than copying a clay-site detail.

whyUs.title: Heathland constraints handled early
whyUs.body: On plots near Chobham Common, Bagshot Heath and Windsor Great Park, we factor in the Thames Basin Heaths protections before design begins, so consent is not put at risk.

whyUs.title: Gardens treated as part of the build
whyUs.body: On the large wooded grounds common here, we plan landscaping alongside the house so paths, levels, planting and drainage work as one scheme across heath and woodland.

whyUs.title: Suited to the prestige market
whyUs.body: We are set up for the detached and gated homes of SL5, from basements and leisure spaces to full replacement dwellings on premium plots, finished to the standard the area expects.

intro: Beaconsfield is a short run from our Harefield base, just over the Buckinghamshire border, so we are on site quickly and back again when a job needs a second look. We work across both halves of the town: the Georgian Old Town crossroads with its listed frontages and conservation rules, and the Edwardian and interwar detached houses of the New Town around the station. Most of our work here is whole-house renovation, house extensions and the landscaping that ties a finished home to its plot.

We deal with Buckinghamshire Council on your behalf, from listed building and conservation area consent in the Old Town to the Green Belt limits that shape what can be built on the larger plots. Beaconsfield is a high-spec market and we build to suit it, with materials and finishes chosen to match the house and the street. You get one contractor managing design, planning and the build through to the garden.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: The Old Town around the historic crossroads: Georgian brick facades, former coaching inns and mock-Tudor, with a high density of listed buildings inside the conservation area.
The New Town near the 1906 station: substantial Edwardian, interwar and modern detached houses in the Metroland tradition, much of it cavity brick.
Large detached, high-spec stock throughout, in one of the wealthiest towns in the country, where a great many homes sit well above the million-pound mark.
Solid-wall period properties on the older streets, alongside clay-tile roofs and timber-frame structures that need handling on their own terms.

localKnowledge.heading: What Beaconsfield homeowners ask us to solve
points: Sympathetic repair and alteration of Old Town listed frontages and coaching-inn structures, done to conservation standards.
High-end whole-house renovations, extensions, basements and leisure additions for the prime New Town market.
Replacement or new-build dwellings on large plots, worked carefully within Green Belt constraints.
Damp and insulation retrofit in solid-wall period stock, and limescale damage from the area's very hard chalk water.

whyUs.title: Old Town listed work
whyUs.body: We repair and alter Georgian frontages and coaching-inn structures to the standard the Old Town conservation area demands, not a generic refurbishment.

whyUs.title: Two stocks, handled distinctly
whyUs.body: Listed Georgian brick and Metroland detached houses are built differently, and we approach each on its own terms rather than treating the whole town the same.

whyUs.title: Green Belt and consent
whyUs.body: We handle Buckinghamshire Council on Green Belt plots and conservation consent, so a replacement dwelling or large extension is shaped to what will actually pass.

whyUs.title: Spec to match the street
whyUs.body: Beaconsfield is among Britain's most affluent towns, and we build and finish to that level, choosing materials that hold their own on a million-pound road.

intro: Bracknell is a New Town, and its homes show it. Most of what we work on here was built between the 1950s and the early 2000s, from the planned estates at Priestwood, Easthampstead, Bullbrook and Harmans Water through to the newer private developments around the regenerated town centre. We run regularly to Bracknell from our Harefield base, a straightforward drive across West London and into Berkshire, so our team and our suppliers reach a job easily and a project stays properly supervised from groundwork to finish.

The work here is modern-home work. Homeowners come to us to extend, reconfigure and lift the standard of post-war and late-century estate houses, and three things come up most: garden landscaping, loft conversions and house extensions. We handle the planning side with Bracknell Forest Council ourselves, including the drainage and ecology points that matter on the wooded fringes near the Thames Basin Heaths, so you are not left to chase consents on your own.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: This is the youngest housing in our catchment. Bracknell was designated a New Town in 1949, so the bulk of the stock is 1950s to 1980s estate housing rather than period property.
The big planned estates — Priestwood, Easthampstead, Bullbrook, Harmans Water — are mostly cavity brick, with system-built elements and a mix of flat and pitched roofs in places.
Around the town centre and its edges there is newer private development in modern brick, alongside the recent Lexicon regeneration.
A small Old Bracknell core survives, but there is little conservation-led stock here. This is a modern-home market, and we build for it accordingly.

localKnowledge.heading: What Bracknell homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extending and reconfiguring compact estate layouts — rear and side extensions, and loft conversions that turn a tight three-bed into a workable family home.
Updating dated 1950s to 1970s construction: tired flat roofs, system-built sections and the thermal upgrades that bring an older estate house up to standard.
Garden transformations on estate plots, with the soakaway and surface-water drainage that the sandy ground needs designed in properly.
Limescale and its damage to bathrooms and heating, a constant on the hard Thames Valley water supply, dealt with as part of any kitchen or bathroom fit-out.

whyUs.title: We build for New Town stock
whyUs.body: Bracknell's housing is post-war and modern, so our work here is extending, reconfiguring and upgrading 1950s-to-2000s estate homes rather than restoring period property.

whyUs.title: We know the ground
whyUs.body: Bracknell sits on free-draining Bagshot sand and gravel, low-shrink ground that is favourable for foundations, though it makes considered soakaway and drainage design essential on every extension and garden scheme.

whyUs.title: Drainage and ecology handled
whyUs.body: On the wooded fringes near the Thames Basin Heaths protected area we factor surface-water and ecology requirements into the design from the start, so consent is not held up later.

whyUs.title: Bracknell Forest planning, managed
whyUs.body: We prepare and run applications through Bracknell Forest Council ourselves, so the planning and drainage detail on your extension or loft is dealt with before work begins.

intro: Hangard Construction works across Burnham from our base in Harefield, a short run south through the Colne Valley that keeps a foreman on site without fuss. Burnham is a South Bucks village of period cottages and Georgian frontages around the conservation high street, with substantial Victorian, interwar and detached commuter homes reaching out towards Burnham Beeches. We build loft conversions, house extensions and fitted kitchens here, and we plan each job around the stock it sits in rather than a standard template.

Most work in Burnham touches planning in some form, so we handle the submissions to Buckinghamshire Council ourselves — Green Belt limits, conservation consent on the high street, and the ecological controls that apply to plots near the Beeches. We know how to frame an extension or a loft to win consent and still do what the home needs. From first drawings to a finished kitchen, you deal with one builder who knows the ground and the rules that come with it.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Period cottages and Georgian and Victorian frontages around the conservation high street, many in brick, flint and timber frame with clay-tile roofs.
Detached and semi-detached Victorian and interwar houses on generous plots, with cavity-brick walls and room to extend.
Larger detached commuter homes toward Burnham Beeches, set on free-draining chalk and clay-with-flints high ground.
Solid-wall older stock that needs careful damp and insulation retrofit rather than off-the-shelf measures.

localKnowledge.heading: What Burnham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions and loft conversions on detached homes that have to sit within Green Belt limits and still gain consent.
Plots adjoining the Burnham Beeches SAC, where tree-root protection and drainage and runoff controls shape the build.
Period repair and alteration in the high-street conservation core, done to a standard that satisfies heritage consent.
Limescale and system wear from very hard chalk water, addressed when we refit kitchens, bathrooms and pipework.

whyUs.title: We build beside the Beeches correctly
whyUs.body: On plots adjoining the Burnham Beeches SAC we work to tree-root protection and drainage and runoff controls, so an extension near ancient woodland is built without harming what the reserve protects.

whyUs.title: Green Belt extensions that gain consent
whyUs.body: We design extensions and loft conversions for Burnham's detached homes to sit within Green Belt limits, and we make the case to Buckinghamshire Council ourselves.

whyUs.title: Conservation-core repair
whyUs.body: On the high street we repair and alter period brick, flint and timber-frame frontages to a standard that holds up under conservation-area consent.

whyUs.title: Built for hard chalk water
whyUs.body: Burnham sits on chalk and the water is very hard, so we specify and protect kitchen and bathroom systems against limescale when we fit them.

intro: Hangard Construction works across Central London from our base in Harefield, a straight run down the A40 and Westway into Westminster and Kensington & Chelsea. We are a main contractor for prime and commercial property, and most of what we do here falls into three areas: property maintenance for portfolio and private owners, basement conversions beneath townhouses and mansion blocks, and house extensions where the footprint and the planning regime allow. Almost every address we touch sits in a conservation area, is listed, or both, so we plan each job around consent from the outset.

We handle the planning side directly with the City of Westminster and RBKC, the strictest authorities in the country, including mandatory pre-application advice and listed-building consent. We are used to building in dense, high-footfall streets where access, working hours, hoarding and party-wall matters all have to be managed before a single tool comes out. The work is exacting and the standards are high, which suits the way we build.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Grand Georgian and Victorian estates in Belgravia, Mayfair, Pimlico and Marylebone, built in solid masonry with stucco render, grand sash windows and ornate plasterwork.
Mansion blocks and tall townhouses, often run as lateral conversions or reinstated from offices back to single residences.
Slate and lead roofs over protected frontages, where restoration matters more than replacement.
Very little post-war housing in the prime cores, so this is a refurbishment and fit-out market rather than new-build.

localKnowledge.heading: What Central London homeowners ask us to solve
points: Listed-building and conservation consent on virtually every project, which we plan for from the first drawing rather than discover later.
Basements dug beneath townhouses and mansion blocks to add space where the footprint cannot grow outward.
Facade, roof and sash restoration on protected frontages that have to be kept faithful to the original.
Riverside damp and flood detailing on tidal-Thames frontages, and limescale from very hard water across the boroughs.

whyUs.title: Consent-led from day one
whyUs.body: With nearly every prime address listed or in a conservation area, we build the listed-building and conservation consent into the programme before we start, working pre-app with Westminster and RBKC.

whyUs.title: Basements under prime stock
whyUs.body: We dig and underpin basements beneath townhouses and mansion blocks on London Clay and Thames gravels, the practical way to add space when the footprint cannot grow.

whyUs.title: Built for dense central streets
whyUs.body: We manage access, restricted hours, hoarding and party-wall agreements on high-footfall streets where there is no room to spread out, the part suburban builders rarely have to plan for.

whyUs.title: Heritage frontages kept faithful
whyUs.body: We restore stucco, sash windows, ornate plasterwork and slate and lead roofs to match the original, so protected facades pass the authorities and read as they always did.

intro: Hangard Construction works across the Chalfonts, from the old village cores of Chalfont St Peter and St Giles to the substantial detached houses set along the Misbourne valley. We are based in Harefield, a short drive south, which keeps our teams close and our supervision regular rather than occasional. Most of what we do here is home renovation, extension and landscaping work, on houses where the finish has to match the standing of the street.

The Chalfonts sit on the edge of the Chilterns AONB and within the Green Belt, so what you can build is shaped by where you build it. We handle the planning side with Buckinghamshire Council, from pre-application advice through to listed building and conservation consent where the village setting requires it. That means a single point of contact who understands both the design you want and the constraints the valley and the green belt place on it.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Listed and period cottages in the conservation cores of Chalfont St Peter and St Giles, built in timber frame, brick and flint under clay-tile roofs.
Substantial detached Edwardian and interwar houses in cavity brick, set back along the valley and the surrounding lanes.
Later detached commuter homes with room to extend, where the brief is usually more space and a better-quality interior.
Houses near the village greens and Milton's Cottage in St Giles, where any external change has to read as part of the historic setting.

localKnowledge.heading: What Chalfont homeowners ask us to solve
points: Flood and groundwater resilience on valley-floor plots, where the Misbourne chalk stream has caused documented flooding in both villages when flows return.
Careful repair of solid-wall and timber-framed cottages, with damp and insulation retrofit done in a way the fabric can tolerate.
Extensions, basements and full renovations on detached homes, planned to sit within AONB and Green Belt limits.
Hard-water problems from the chalk aquifer, including limescale damage to boilers and heating systems.

whyUs.title: Built for the Misbourne valley
whyUs.body: We plan valley-floor renovations and extensions around the risk of chalk-stream flooding and groundwater, with detailing that holds up when the Misbourne runs.

whyUs.title: Conservation core experience
whyUs.body: We repair and extend listed and period cottages in the St Peter and St Giles cores using brick, flint and timber methods the original fabric was built with.

whyUs.title: We carry the planning
whyUs.body: We take extension and basement schemes through Buckinghamshire Council, including the AONB, Green Belt and conservation consent that the Chalfonts so often demand.

whyUs.title: Honest about hard water
whyUs.body: We allow for the area's very hard chalk water when we fit kitchens, bathrooms and heating, protecting the systems from limescale from the day they go in.

intro: Chelsea is close work for us. From our Harefield base we run jobs across SW3 and SW10 daily, so our teams reach Cheyne Walk, the King's Road and the streets off Sloane Square without the day starting late. We build extensions, restore heritage roofs and convert lofts on the stucco terraces and studio houses that give the borough its character, and we work to the standards those buildings deserve.

Almost every project here touches the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, so we plan for it from the outset. We handle conservation-area consent, listed-building applications and party-wall agreements on continuous terraces, and we sequence the work to suit narrow frontages and shared walls. The result is a build that holds up to scrutiny from neighbours and the council alike.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: White stucco-fronted Victorian and Regency terraces, with Georgian townhouses behind solid masonry, sash windows and ornate plasterwork.
Chelsea's distinctive artists' studio houses, with their double-height volumes and large north-light glazing.
Red-brick mansion blocks, and riverside apartments along the Chelsea Embankment near Cheyne Walk.
Almost no post-war stock, and much of it listed or sitting within one of the borough's conservation areas.

localKnowledge.heading: What Chelsea homeowners ask us to solve
points: Single-storey basements dug into London Clay, kept within the RBKC limits on depth and garden footprint, with proper waterproofing.
Stucco facade restoration and damp on solid walls, repaired to conservation standards rather than patched over.
Heritage roofing in slate and lead, and the specialist glazing and roof restoration that studio houses demand.
Listed-building consent, conservation approvals and party-wall agreements on tightly joined terraces, plus limescale from the very hard water.

whyUs.title: Stucco done properly
whyUs.body: We restore stucco render and solid-wall facades using breathable detailing the Royal Borough will accept, so the front stays sound and the damp stays out.

whyUs.title: Basements within the rules
whyUs.body: We dig single-storey basements into Chelsea's London Clay inside RBKC's post-2015 limits on depth and garden cover, with waterproofing specified for the ground.

whyUs.title: Studio houses understood
whyUs.body: We restore and extend the borough's double-height, top-lit artists' studio houses, where the glazing and roof work need a builder who knows the stock.

whyUs.title: Riverside resilience
whyUs.body: On the Chelsea Embankment the ground is Thames alluvium and gravel, wetter and softer than inland, so we detail riverside properties for tidal damp and groundwater.

intro: Chesham is a short run north for us from our Harefield base, up into the Chiltern valleys where the River Chess rises. We work across the whole town, from the period cottages and brick-and-flint properties of the old conservation core to the Victorian and Edwardian terraces, the interwar semis on the slopes, and the modern homes spreading up the surrounding hills. Most of what we are asked for here is renovation, extension and landscaping work that respects the grain of a Chiltern market town rather than fighting it.

We handle the planning side with Buckinghamshire Council ourselves, which matters in Chesham more than most places. Much of the town sits within the Chilterns AONB and the Green Belt, and the old centre carries conservation-area and listed constraints, so getting consent right is half the job. We manage the drawings, the applications and the conditions, then build the renovation, extension or landscaped scheme cleanly through to handover.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: The old-town core: brick, flint and timber-frame buildings with clay-tile roofs, much of it listed or inside the conservation area.
Victorian and Edwardian terraces and villas built out as Chesham grew with the Metropolitan line terminus.
Interwar semis and modern homes climbing the valley sides, mixing solid-wall and cavity construction.
One of the broadest stocks in the Chilterns, from small period cottages to larger detached houses on the hills.

localKnowledge.heading: What Chesham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Flood and groundwater resilience on valley-floor properties near the Chess, the chalk stream that rises in the town and has flooded it before.
Careful repair and alteration of listed and conservation-core homes in brick, flint and timber.
Extensions, loft conversions and renovations worked through AONB and Green Belt limits.
Damp and cold in solid-wall period homes, and limescale damage from the very hard chalk-aquifer water.

whyUs.title: We build for the Chess
whyUs.body: On valley-floor sites near the river we plan extensions and renovations with the chalk stream's groundwater and flood history in mind, rather than treating it as an afterthought.

whyUs.title: Conservation-core experience
whyUs.body: We repair and extend listed and conservation-area homes in brick, flint and timber to the standard Chesham's old town requires.

whyUs.title: AONB and Green Belt planning
whyUs.body: We take Chesham extensions and loft conversions through Buckinghamshire Council inside the Chilterns AONB and Green Belt, where what you can build is tightly defined.

whyUs.title: Solid-wall and hard-water know-how
whyUs.body: We tackle damp and insulation in Chesham's solid-wall period homes and protect systems against the limescale that the town's very hard water brings.

intro: Hangard Construction works across Chiswick from our base in Harefield, a short run down through West London. We know the W4 streets well, from the Victorian and Edwardian terraces off the High Road to the listed Queen Anne Revival houses of Bedford Park and the riverside homes at Chiswick Mall. Loft conversions, house extensions and landscaping are the work we are most often called on for here, and we approach each one with respect for the period and the street it sits on.

Most projects in Chiswick involve the planning and conservation rules of the London Borough of Hounslow, and we handle that process for you from first drawings through to consent. We are used to the tighter controls that come with conservation areas and listed stock, and we detail the work so it reads as part of the house rather than an addition to it. The result is an extension, loft or garden that suits a period family home and holds its value on a sought-after street.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Georgian, Victorian and Edwardian houses, much of it built as Chiswick grew through the 19th century.
Bedford Park, often called the world's first garden suburb, with red-brick Queen Anne Revival houses, tile-hung walls, varied gables and white-painted joinery.
Victorian and Edwardian terraces with rear outriggers, sash windows and slate roofs, well suited to side-return work.
The historic riverside core at Old Chiswick and Chiswick Mall, where tidal-Thames detailing matters.

localKnowledge.heading: What Chiswick homeowners ask us to solve
points: Side-return and rear extensions on terraces, opening up tight Victorian and Edwardian plans for family living.
Loft conversions that add bedrooms and bathrooms without altering the street elevation.
Sympathetic repair and alteration to Bedford Park's listed houses, where external change is tightly controlled.
Movement on London Clay near street trees, riverside damp at the Mall, and limescale from very hard water.

whyUs.title: Conservation-area consent handled
whyUs.body: We prepare and run applications through the London Borough of Hounslow for Chiswick's conservation areas, including the listed Queen Anne Revival houses of Bedford Park.

whyUs.title: Built for period terraces
whyUs.body: We plan side-return and rear extensions around the outriggers, sash windows and slate roofs of W4's Victorian and Edwardian houses so the new work sits naturally.

whyUs.title: Matched materials
whyUs.body: We source red brick, hung tile and joinery to match Bedford Park's distinctive palette, so repairs and additions read as part of the original house.

whyUs.title: Ground and river aware
whyUs.body: We allow for clay shrink-swell near Chiswick's street trees and detail riverside plots at Chiswick Mall for tidal-Thames damp and flood risk.

intro: Chorleywood sits a short drive from our Harefield base, just over the county line on the Chiltern edge. We know its Metroland streets well: the late-Victorian and Edwardian villas that followed the railway, the Arts and Crafts detached homes around the Common, and the generous plots that invite careful extension. Most of our work here is renovation and extension of these period houses, alongside landscaping that settles a home into its garden.

Because the village falls under Three Rivers District Council, planning is a Hertfordshire matter, not Buckinghamshire, and we handle that distinction for you. We prepare applications, manage Green Belt and conservation-area constraints, and see consent through before a tool is lifted. Our work is low volume by design, so the homes we take on get measured attention from start to finish.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Late-Victorian and Edwardian detached villas built for London commuters after the railway arrived in 1889.
Arts and Crafts houses in the Voysey tradition, with distinctive rendering, steep roofs and considered detailing.
Interwar and mock-Tudor detached homes set on large gardens near Chorleywood Common.
Modern executive houses on former estate land, such as the Loudwater plots.

localKnowledge.heading: What Chorleywood homeowners ask us to solve
points: Sympathetic extensions to Arts and Crafts and Metroland villas, matching the original brick, render and roof lines.
Larger rear and side additions, basements and leisure rooms on generous detached plots.
Conservation-area and Green Belt consent for work near Chorleywood Common and across the village.
Damp and insulation retrofit in period houses, and limescale from the very hard chalk water.

whyUs.title: We match the village's architecture
whyUs.body: We work to the detailing of Voysey-era and Metroland villas, so a new extension reads as part of the original house rather than an addition.

whyUs.title: Three Rivers planning, handled
whyUs.body: Chorleywood sits under Three Rivers District Council in Hertfordshire, and we prepare and manage applications to that authority, not Buckinghamshire's.

whyUs.title: Green Belt and conservation experience
whyUs.body: Much of the village lies in Green Belt with a conservation area around the Common, and we shape schemes to win consent within those constraints.

whyUs.title: Built for chalk ground and hard water
whyUs.body: We allow for the chalk and clay-with-flints beneath Chorleywood in our foundations, and specify for the very hard local water that scales pipework and fittings.

intro: Denham sits just across the Colne from our Harefield yard, so it is one of the closest places we work. That proximity matters on a build: it means short site visits, materials on hand, and a foreman who can be on your driveway within minutes rather than half a day. Most of our work here is house extensions, full home renovations and landscaping on the detached commuter homes around Denham Green, Higher Denham and the village edge.

Because Denham falls under Buckinghamshire Council, with the Colne Valley Regional Park and Green Belt overlaying much of the area, we handle the planning side ourselves. We prepare and submit the applications, deal with the conservation and Green Belt constraints, and keep the design within what the authority will pass. From a single-storey rear extension to a reordered ground floor and reworked garden, we run the whole job from drawings to finished landscaping.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Denham Village has a protected conservation core of timber-framed and Georgian cottages, brick and clay-tile roofs, frequently used as a film location and tightly controlled.
Around the village sit large detached commuter homes, many Edwardian, interwar or modern, mostly cavity brick.
Higher Denham and Denham Green are more suburban in character than the historic village, with room for extension and reconfiguration.
Plots near the River Colne and the old gravel workings sit low in the valley on river-terrace gravels and alluvium.

localKnowledge.heading: What Denham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extending and renovating detached homes within Green Belt and Regional Park limits, where the design has to be judged carefully before it goes to the council.
Sensitive heritage repair to listed and timber-framed cottages in the village, worked to a high standard with conservation consent.
Flood and groundwater resilience on low-lying and riverside plots in the Colne Valley floodplain.
Damp and insulation work in solid-wall and timber-framed homes, and limescale from the area's very hard chalk water.

whyUs.title: Minutes from your door
whyUs.body: Denham is the next parish across the Colne from our Harefield base, so site visits, deliveries and snagging happen quickly rather than being booked days ahead.

whyUs.title: Green Belt and Regional Park planning
whyUs.body: We design extensions to sit within Buckinghamshire Council's Green Belt and Colne Valley Regional Park limits, and prepare the application so it has a fair chance of consent.

whyUs.title: Conservation-grade village work
whyUs.body: We repair and extend the timber-framed and Georgian cottages of Denham's protected village core to the heritage standard the listing demands.

whyUs.title: Building on valley ground
whyUs.body: On low-lying plots over the Colne floodplain's river-terrace gravels and alluvium, we detail foundations and drainage for groundwater and flood risk.

intro: Ealing earned its name as the Queen of the Suburbs, and the streets bear it out: long rows of Victorian and Edwardian terraces and villas, sash windows, slate roofs and small original kitchens crying out for more room. We work across W5 and W13 from our base in Harefield, a short run down through the Home Counties edge of West London, so we are on site early and our team and trades are never far away. Loft conversions and side-return house extensions make up the bulk of what we are asked for here, alongside considered landscaping that ties a reworked rear elevation back to the garden.

Period homes in Ealing come with real constraints, and we plan for them from the first visit. Many streets carry Article 4 directions and sit within conservation areas such as Ealing Green and St Stephen's, so works that would be permitted development elsewhere need full consent. We prepare and submit the planning and conservation applications to the London Borough of Ealing ourselves, detail the work sympathetically, and keep the build honest to the age of the house.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Predominantly Victorian and Edwardian stock: terraces, villas and large period houses around St Stephen's and Pitshanger.
Solid or early-cavity brickwork on shallow footings, with suspended timber floors and sash windows throughout.
Rear outriggers beside a narrow side return, the classic terrace layout that opens up so well for a kitchen extension.
Slate roofs and original detailing that reward careful repair rather than wholesale replacement.

localKnowledge.heading: What Ealing homeowners ask us to solve
points: Side-return and rear kitchen extensions to replace cramped original kitchens and bring in light from the garden.
Loft conversions to add bedrooms and bathrooms, where demand across W5 and W13 is consistently strong.
Subsidence and heave cracking on shallow period footings, driven by London Clay and mature street trees.
Full planning and conservation consent on Article 4 streets where permitted-development rights have been removed.

whyUs.title: London Clay, planned for
whyUs.body: Ealing sits on shrink-swell London Clay, so we assess shallow period footings and nearby street trees and underpin or detail foundations properly before adding load.

whyUs.title: Article 4 handled in-house
whyUs.body: We know which Ealing streets carry Article 4 directions and prepare full planning applications to the borough where neighbouring areas would only need permitted development.

whyUs.title: Conservation-area detailing
whyUs.body: On protected streets such as Ealing Green and St Stephen's we match brick, slate and sash detailing so a side-return or loft reads as part of the original house.

whyUs.title: Built for the terrace layout
whyUs.body: We work the narrow side return and rear outrigger common to Ealing terraces, turning small original kitchens into open, well-lit ground floors.

intro: Eastcote sits a short drive from our Harefield base, so we are on site quickly and back again when a detail needs checking. It is a Metroland suburb of interwar semis and detached homes, and most of our work here is the everyday craft those houses ask for: new kitchens, reworked bathrooms and loft conversions that add a bedroom under the existing roofline. We know the streets between Ruislip and Pinner, and we know how these homes are built.

We handle planning and building control with the London Borough of Hillingdon, and where a project touches the Eastcote Village Conservation Area we manage consent on that footing too. The conservation area spans both Hillingdon and Harrow, a quirk of this village that we navigate as a matter of routine. From first survey to final fix, you deal with builders who understand the local stock rather than a contractor passing through.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Interwar semis and detached houses make up most of the stock: cavity brick, clay tile roofs and mock-Tudor detailing, well suited to rear extensions and loft conversions.
Older brick and timber village cottages around the historic core, where listed and conservation constraints shape what can be done.
Family Metroland streets laid out between Ruislip and Pinner, leafy and generously plotted, with room to extend at the rear.
Eastcote House and its gardens anchor the village character that newer work is expected to sit alongside.

localKnowledge.heading: What Eastcote homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions and loft conversions on interwar semis and detached homes, adding space without losing the proportions of the original house.
Period repair and sympathetic work inside the Eastcote Village Conservation Area, which carries consent across both Hillingdon and Harrow.
London Clay subsidence near mature street trees, where shallow footings need careful attention before any extension goes ahead.
Kitchen and bathroom fit-outs across the suburban stock, often with measures to manage the area's very hard water and limescale.

whyUs.title: Built for interwar stock
whyUs.body: We work daily on Eastcote's cavity-brick semis and detached homes, so rear extensions and loft conversions are matched to how these houses were actually built.

whyUs.title: Two-borough conservation consent
whyUs.body: The Eastcote Village Conservation Area straddles Hillingdon and Harrow, and we manage consent across that boundary rather than treating it as one tidy authority.

whyUs.title: Clay and tree-aware groundwork
whyUs.body: London Clay shrinks and swells around the mature street trees here, so we check footings and ground before committing to an extension design.

whyUs.title: Close from Harefield
whyUs.body: Our base is a short drive away, which means quick surveys, prompt fixes and a site presence that does not depend on a long commute.

intro: Farnham Common sits a short drive from our Harefield base, just across the M40 and M25 to the north-west, so we are on site quickly and back again without losing a day to travel. It is a village of large detached homes on generous, wooded plots, set against Burnham Beeches and held within the Green Belt. We work on that kind of house every week: full renovations of Edwardian and interwar properties, extensions that suit a leafy plot, and garden landscaping that respects the mature trees around it. The work is unhurried and properly supervised, with one point of contact from first survey to handover.

Most projects here turn on the setting as much as the building. We handle the planning side with Buckinghamshire Council, including Green Belt limits, the constraints of building near the Burnham Beeches SAC, and Tree Preservation Orders on established gardens. Foundations and drainage are designed around root protection and the chalk and clay-with-flints ground beneath, not bolted on as an afterthought. Whether it is a measured renovation, a single- or double-storey extension, or a new garden, we plan it for a wooded South Bucks plot from the outset.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Large detached houses on generous, often wooded plots, the defining stock of this affluent South Bucks village beside Burnham Beeches.
A mix of substantial Edwardian and interwar properties alongside later modern detached homes, rather than terraces or a listed core.
Cavity brickwork with clay-tile roofs, set in mature gardens that frequently carry their own established trees.
A village-centre character around the Farnham Common parade and Farnham Royal, with proximity to ancient woodland shaping how each plot can be built on.

localKnowledge.heading: What Farnham Common homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions, basements and leisure additions to large detached homes, designed to use a generous plot without crowding the trees on it.
Foundation and drainage design on wooded plots near the Burnham Beeches SAC, where root protection governs how and where you build.
Garden landscaping that works around mature trees and likely Tree Preservation Orders, rather than fighting them.
Green Belt planning constraints on extensions and replacement dwellings, and the limescale that comes with very hard chalk-fed water.

whyUs.title: Built for wooded plots
whyUs.body: We design foundations and drainage around tree-root protection and the constraints of building near the Burnham Beeches SAC, the issue that defines work in this village.

whyUs.title: Green Belt planning, handled
whyUs.body: We take extensions, basements and replacement dwellings through Buckinghamshire Council within Green Belt limits, so the scheme is shaped to pass before a spade goes in.

whyUs.title: At home with large detached houses
whyUs.body: Renovating and extending substantial Edwardian, interwar and modern detached homes on generous plots is our routine work, not an occasional job.

whyUs.title: Gardens that respect the trees
whyUs.body: Our landscaping is planned around mature trees and likely Tree Preservation Orders, with hard-water protection specified for systems fed by very hard chalk water.

intro: Hangard Construction works across Fulham from our base in Harefield, an easy run down into SW6 and the streets around Bishop's Park, Hurlingham and the Thames riverside. Most of our work here is on the classic Fulham terrace, the long Victorian and Edwardian rows with rear outriggers that lend themselves so well to side-return and rear kitchen extensions, loft conversions and, where the ground allows, basement conversions. We know how these houses are built, from the shallow footings and suspended timber floors to the slate roofs, and we plan each job around what the structure can actually take.

We handle the planning side with the London Borough of Hammersmith & Fulham, including the Article 4 directions and conservation consent that apply on many of the borough's protected streets. That groundwork matters here, because so much of Fulham sits within one of its conservation areas. From first drawings to final coat, you deal with a single team that builds to a high standard and keeps the site clean and considerate in a tight terraced setting.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Victorian and Edwardian terraces are the backbone of Fulham, built in solid brick on shallow footings with suspended timber floors, sash windows and rear outriggers.
Those uniform terraced streets, with their rear projections, make Fulham prime territory for side-return extensions and loft conversions.
Slate roofs are the norm on the period stock and need careful, like-for-like work to keep the streetscape intact.
The riverside adds a different layer, with mansion flats and 1970s and 80s blocks whose flat roofs are prone to failure.

localKnowledge.heading: What Fulham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Side-return and rear kitchen extensions on the Victorian terraces, the defining local job, opening up dark, narrow ground floors into a single living space.
Loft conversions on terraces and outriggers, where homeowners need another bedroom or bathroom without moving.
Subsidence on the London Clay, made worse by mature street trees and shallow footings, and separately on the softer Thames alluvial ground near the river.
Conservation and Article 4 consent on protected SW6 streets, plus the everyday damp in solid-brick walls and limescale from very hard water.

whyUs.title: Built for the Fulham terrace
whyUs.body: We work day in, day out on Victorian and Edwardian terraces with rear outriggers, so side-return extensions and loft conversions are routine ground for us, not a one-off.

whyUs.title: Footings read for the clay
whyUs.body: We plan and underpin with London Clay shrink-swell and nearby street trees in mind, and treat the softer Thames alluvial ground near the river as its own subsidence risk.

whyUs.title: Conservation and Article 4 handled
whyUs.body: We deal with Hammersmith & Fulham's many conservation areas and Article 4 streets, getting the right consent before work starts so the project stands up to scrutiny.

whyUs.title: Honest about the period fabric
whyUs.body: We address the damp common in solid-brick terraces and the flat-roof failures on the riverside blocks at the root, rather than papering over them.

intro: Gerrards Cross is a detached-house town. It grew after the 1906 railway into one of the country's most affluent commuter communities, and the streets around the high street, the Common and East Common hold large Edwardian, interwar and modern executive homes on generous plots. We work on these properties regularly, and our base in Harefield is a short drive south, so a site visit, a delivery or a foreman's morning check is straightforward to arrange. Home renovations, house extensions and premium driveways are the work we are asked for most here.

Most of what we build in Gerrards Cross sits within the Green Belt, where what you can add is carefully bounded. We handle the planning with Buckinghamshire Council on your behalf, from pre-application discussion through to conditions, and we know how the authority treats extensions, replacement dwellings and outbuildings on these plots. The result is a single team running the renovation, the extension or the new driveway and patio from first drawing to final clean.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Large detached houses on generous plots — the town is a premium detached market rather than a terraced or listed old-town core.
Edwardian and interwar villas built up after the 1906 railway, many with mock-Tudor and Arts-and-Crafts detailing.
Cavity-brick construction with clay-tile roofs, alongside substantial modern executive builds.
Mature, sizeable gardens that lend themselves to extensions, leisure additions and high-end landscaping.

localKnowledge.heading: What Gerrards Cross homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions, basements and leisure spaces — pools, gyms and garaging — on the large plots the town is known for.
Whole-house renovation and sympathetic modernisation of Edwardian and interwar villas without losing their character.
Replacement dwellings and high-spec rebuilds worked carefully within Green Belt limits.
Limescale and the steady wear of very hard chalk water on bathrooms, kitchens and heating systems.

whyUs.title: Built for the detached-house market
whyUs.body: Our work here is large extensions, basements and leisure additions on generous plots, not the terraced and side-return jobs of inner London.

whyUs.title: Green Belt planning, handled
whyUs.body: We take extensions, outbuildings and replacement dwellings through Buckinghamshire Council and know how the Green Belt shapes what can be approved.

whyUs.title: Founded on chalk and clay-with-flints
whyUs.body: We design foundations and basements for the town's free-draining chalk with its clay-with-flints capping, allowing for the localised ground movement it can bring.

whyUs.title: Specified for very hard water
whyUs.body: Renovations and new bathrooms here are detailed with limescale in mind, from fittings and surfaces to the protection of heating and hot-water systems.

intro: Harefield is our home town and our head office sits here, so a call out to a job in the village or its lanes is a short drive, not a motorway trek. We work the full length of the High Street and the streets around it, from period cottages in the conservation area to the interwar and post-war homes that grew up around the old core. Loft conversions, house extensions and new kitchens are the work we are asked for most here, and we treat each one as a piece of the village rather than a standard product.

Being based in Harefield means we know the London Borough of Hillingdon as a planning authority, and we handle the applications, drawings and conservation-area and Green Belt consents that this part of UB9 demands. We understand the chalk-and-gravel ground underfoot, the very hard water, and the floodplain that runs along the Colne and the Grand Union Canal. That local knowledge shapes how we design a loft, set out an extension or fit a kitchen, so the result suits the house and clears the council the first time.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Period cottages and two- and three-storey brick and rendered buildings along the High Street, many inside the Harefield Village Conservation Area, with solid single-skin walls, timber floors and slate or clay-tile roofs.
Interwar and post-war suburban houses that surround the old core, built with cavity walls, strip or concrete foundations and clay-tile roofs, as Harefield grew from rural parish to commuter village.
Larger detached homes, farmhouses and smallholdings on the semi-rural fringe, set in the Colne Valley and Green Belt rather than dense terraced streets.
Far less Victorian terracing than neighbouring Uxbridge or Ruislip, so Harefield reads as a village and asks for sympathetic, house-by-house work rather than repeat formulas.

localKnowledge.heading: What Harefield homeowners ask us to solve
points: Limescale damage to boilers, cylinders and pipework from the very hard chalk-fed water, often replaced alongside a new kitchen or bathroom fit-out.
Cold, damp and condensation in solid-wall period cottages with no cavity and little insulation, put right with careful retrofit rather than off-the-shelf cladding.
Worn slate and clay-tile roofs and tired chimney stacks on the older village stock, repaired or recovered in keeping with the conservation area.
Planning friction on extensions, lofts and outbuildings where conservation-area consent and Green Belt policy both apply, which we navigate with Hillingdon before a spade goes in.

whyUs.title: Based in Harefield, not passing through
whyUs.body: Our head office and yard are in the village, so site visits, snagging and the day-to-day of a build happen quickly because we are minutes away, not hours.

whyUs.title: We build for chalk and gravel
whyUs.body: Harefield ground sits on Chiltern chalk and Colne Valley river-terrace gravels rather than London Clay, so we set foundations to the conditions we actually find here instead of assuming inner-London shrink-swell.

whyUs.title: Conservation and Green Belt handled
whyUs.body: We design lofts and extensions that respect the Harefield Village Conservation Area and Green Belt limits, choosing windows, roofs and materials that Hillingdon will pass.

whyUs.title: Hard water and floodplain understood
whyUs.body: We specify wet systems and kitchens to cope with the area's very hard water, and add tanking, raised levels and drainage on the Colne-side and canal-side plots that carry flood risk.

intro: Hatch End is the north-eastern edge of Pinner, a Metroland garden suburb that grew up around the railway in the 1920s and 30s. The homes here are interwar semis and detached houses, mostly cavity brick with mock-Tudor gables, bay windows and clay-tile roofs. We are a short run from our Harefield base, so we know these streets and the way these houses are built. Most of our work here is loft conversions and rear extensions on Metroland semis, with kitchen fitting close behind.

We handle the planning side with the London Borough of Harrow, including the Article 4 directions that apply on some streets and the householder applications that govern lofts and extensions. We design to suit the period, so a new roofline or rear addition reads as part of the house rather than bolted on. We allow for the local ground too, as London Clay underlies most of Harrow and brings its own demands on footings. From first drawings to a swept site, you deal with the same team throughout.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Interwar semis and detached houses from the 1920s and 30s garden-estate expansion, the defining Metroland stock of Hatch End.
Cavity brick construction with mock-Tudor detailing, bay windows and clay-tile roofs, all of which take well to loft and rear-extension work.
Pockets of Edwardian houses and later modern infill sit alongside the interwar majority on the leafier roads.
Generous plots and original roof spaces that suit dormer and hip-to-gable lofts and full-width rear additions.

localKnowledge.heading: What Hatch End homeowners ask us to solve
points: Loft conversions and rear extensions on Metroland semis, the most common job on these streets, to add bedrooms and open up the kitchen.
Kitchen and bathroom fit-outs across the interwar stock, often paired with a new extension.
Movement and cracking from London Clay shrink-swell, made worse by mature street trees and shallow original footings.
Damp and ground-water awareness on plots near the River Pinn around Headstone Lane, and limescale protection against the very hard local water.

whyUs.title: Built for Metroland semis
whyUs.body: We work day in, day out on Hatch End's 1920s and 30s cavity-brick semis, so a loft dormer or rear extension is detailed to match the mock-Tudor gables and clay-tile roofs rather than fight them.

whyUs.title: We design for London Clay
whyUs.body: London Clay underlies most of Harrow and shrinks and swells with the seasons, so we size footings for the ground and for the mature street trees nearby instead of assuming a standard depth.

whyUs.title: River Pinn ground awareness
whyUs.body: On plots near the River Pinn around Headstone Lane we plan for damp and higher ground water, with tanking and drainage detailed before the work starts rather than chased afterwards.

whyUs.title: Harrow planning handled
whyUs.body: We prepare and run householder applications through the London Borough of Harrow and check for Article 4 directions on your street, so loft and extension consent is dealt with properly from the outset.

intro: Holland Park is one of London's most particular places to build. The stucco villas and grand terraces of the Ladbroke and Holland Park estates demand whole-house thinking, not single-room fixes, and we work to that standard. From our Harefield base it is a straightforward run east into W11 and W14, so we keep a settled crew on site rather than passing through, and we hold the day-to-day discipline these streets need around dust, deliveries and noise.

Most of what we do here is basement work beneath grand villas and the long-running maintenance that large prime homes need to stay right. We handle planning and conservation consent with the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, including the basement caps and garden limits that apply across the borough's conservation areas. Loft conversions, facade restoration and discreet services upgrades follow the same approach: careful drawings, named contacts, and respect for the neighbours and garden trustees on either side.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Large Victorian stucco villas and grand terraces of the Ladbroke and Holland Park estates, many arranged around shared private garden squares.
Solid masonry behind the stucco, with large floorplates, sash windows, slate and lead roofs, and ornate plasterwork to protect.
Substantial detached and semi-detached mansions, plus high-end mansion flats, where a full refurbishment is the norm rather than a single job.
Deep basements dug into London Clay are common, adding gyms, pools and cinemas beneath the original footprint.

localKnowledge.heading: What Holland Park homeowners ask us to solve
points: Whole-house deep refurbishment with a single-storey basement, kept within RBKC's caps on depth and garden coverage.
Stucco facade and render restoration with proper damp control, finished to conservation standards.
Works next to communal garden squares, where access, trustee consent and tree and root protection all have to be managed.
Listed-building and party-wall consent in grand terraces, and discreet M&E upgrades for pools, climate and cinema rooms in very large homes.

whyUs.title: Basements built for London Clay
whyUs.body: We engineer single-storey basements into the clay beneath these villas with proper groundwater control, kept within the borough's depth and garden limits.

whyUs.title: We work around the garden squares
whyUs.body: On houses backing onto shared squares we plan access, secure trustee consent and protect mature trees and roots before a tool comes out.

whyUs.title: Stucco kept right
whyUs.body: We restore stucco facades and render and control the damp behind them to the conservation standards the Ladbroke and Holland Park areas require.

whyUs.title: Maintenance for large prime homes
whyUs.body: We hold long-term care of substantial W11 and W14 houses, from limescale-driven hard-water issues to discreet upgrades of services across big floorplates.

intro: Ickenham sits right on our doorstep. From our Harefield base it is only a few minutes up the road, which makes us about as local as a main contractor gets here. We know the village green, the old pump and St Giles' church, the interwar Metroland semis that fan out from the Swakeleys development, and the way the River Pinn runs quietly through the area. That familiarity shows in how we plan a kitchen, a bathroom or an extension on these homes.

Most of our work in Ickenham is kitchen and bathroom fit-outs and house extensions on the suburban stock. We handle the planning and building control side with the London Borough of Hillingdon ourselves, so you are not left chasing drawings or conditions. Where a project sits near the conservation core around the green and church, we work to the constraints rather than against them, and we keep the site tidy because we are rarely more than a short drive away if anything needs attention.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Interwar Metroland semis and detached houses from the Swakeleys era, built in cavity brick with clay tile roofs and mock-Tudor detailing.
A small conservation core around the village green, the pump and St Giles' church, with older brick and timber and listed fabric.
Later 20th-century housing across the wider suburb, well suited to rear extensions and loft conversions.
Proximity to the Jacobean Swakeleys House means some plots carry genuine heritage sensitivity to respect.

localKnowledge.heading: What Ickenham homeowners ask us to solve
points: Rear and side extensions and loft conversions on interwar semis and detached homes, adding space without losing the period look.
Kitchen and bathroom fit-outs across the suburban stock, often the first job people bring us.
Limescale and fittings worn by very hard water, which we plan around when specifying bathrooms and kitchens.
Footings and cracking on London Clay, made worse by mature street trees, plus damp on lower ground near the River Pinn.

whyUs.body: Ickenham directly neighbours our Harefield base, so we are on site within minutes and back again quickly if anything needs a second look.

whyUs.title: Right for the village core
whyUs.body: Around the green, the pump and St Giles' church we work to Hillingdon's conservation constraints and the heritage setting of nearby Swakeleys House.

whyUs.title: Specified for hard water
whyUs.body: We choose taps, valves and finishes that stand up to Ickenham's very hard water, so a new kitchen or bathroom is not spoilt by limescale within a year.

intro: Iver sits just across the Colne from our Harefield base, so we are on site here within minutes rather than hours. It is a Colne Valley Green Belt village, with a period core in Iver and Iver Heath, the 1920s garden-suburb streets of Richings Park, and interwar and modern detached homes spread between them. We build extensions, take on full home renovations and reshape gardens with new landscaping, working to the grain of each house rather than against it.

Every project here meets Green Belt and Colne Valley Regional Park constraints, and we prepare and submit the planning case to Buckinghamshire Council ourselves. We know where the limits bite in this part of the valley and design within them from the first sketch. Being this close means clear scheduling, regular site visits and a settled crew for the length of the job.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Period cottages in the Iver and Iver Heath cores, built in brick, flint, timber and clay tile, some with conservation or listing constraints.
Richings Park, a distinct 1920s garden-suburb estate where renovations need to stay sympathetic to the original character.
Interwar and modern semis and detached houses in cavity brick across the village.
Generally affluent commuter stock, with the room and ambition for considered extensions and full refurbishments.

localKnowledge.heading: What Iver homeowners ask us to solve
points: Extensions and renovations kept within Green Belt and Regional Park limits, with the planning case made carefully to Buckinghamshire Council.
Foundations on worked and made ground left by the valley's gravel extraction, where the ground needs proper investigation before a spade goes in.
Flood and groundwater risk on low-lying plots near the River Colne and the old gravel-pit lakes.
Limescale and system wear from very hard water, and damp or insulation retrofit in older houses.

whyUs.title: Built for worked ground

whyUs.title: Right for the period stock
whyUs.body: From Iver's flint-and-tile cottages to Richings Park's 1920s houses, we renovate in keeping with the original character and materials.

intro: Kensington is a short run from our Harefield base, and we work across the Royal Borough's grand stucco terraces, red-brick mansions and garden squares. Most of what we do here sits within tight heritage limits, so we plan carefully and build cleanly. Basement conversions, loft conversions and roofing are the work homeowners ask us for most, and each one starts with the borough's planning regime, not against it.

Almost the whole of Kensington falls under conservation control, with thousands of listed buildings and a strict basement policy. We handle the consent process with the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, prepare the structural method statements a basement dig needs, and keep neighbours and party-wall matters in order on continuous terraces. The result is work that holds up to the borough's standards and to the value of the house it sits in.

localKnowledge.heading: The homes we work on here
points: Grand white stucco Regency and Victorian terraces, with solid masonry behind lime render that needs ongoing upkeep.
Red-brick Queen Anne and Pont Street Dutch mansions, and formal garden squares throughout W8 and SW7.
Edwardian mansion blocks with shared structure, period services and real lateral-conversion potential.
Ornate cornicing, sash windows and slate-and-lead roofs, with almost no post-war stock to speak of.

localKnowledge.heading: What Kensington homeowners ask us to solve
points: Single-storey basements dug into London Clay, designed within the Royal Borough's post-2015 limit of one storey under no more than half the garden.
Stucco facade cracking, damp and render failure on solid walls, restored with lime render rather than patched over.
Listed-building and conservation consent for almost any external change, across the borough's many conservation areas.
Party-wall complexity on continuous stucco terraces, and limescale from very hard Thames water on heating and wet systems.

whyUs.title: Compliant basements, not banned double-digs

whyUs.title: Stucco done in lime, not cement

whyUs.title: Conservation consent handled
